words.exampleNL: Op maandag begin ik weer met werken.
words.exampleEN: On Monday I start working again.
words.audioHint: Long 'aa' sound. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: On Tuesday I have an appointment.
words.audioHint: Short 'i' sound. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Op woensdag zijn de kinderen vrij.
words.exampleEN: On Wednesday the children are off.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo' in 'moon'. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Donderdag ga ik naar de markt.
words.exampleEN: On Thursday I go to the market.
words.audioHint: Short 'o' sound. Three syllables. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: Friday is my favorite day.
words.audioHint: The 'ij' sounds like 'eye'. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Op zaterdag ga ik boodschappen doen.
words.exampleEN: On Saturday I go grocery shopping.
words.audioHint: Short 'a'. The 'z' is softer than in English.

words.exampleEN: Sunday is a day of rest.
words.audioHint: Short 'o' sound. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: In januari is het koud in Nederland.
words.exampleEN: In January it is cold in the Netherlands.
words.audioHint: The 'j' sounds like English 'y'. Stress on the third syllable.

words.exampleEN: February is the shortest month.
words.audioHint: Similar to English. Stress on the third syllable.

words.exampleEN: Spring begins in March.
words.audioHint: Long 'aa' sound. Rolled 'r'.

words.exampleNL: In april kan het weer heel wisselend zijn.
words.exampleEN: In April the weather can be very changeable.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: a-PRIL.

words.exampleEN: In May the flowers bloom.
words.audioHint: Sounds like English 'my'.

words.exampleEN: Summer begins in June.
words.audioHint: The 'j' sounds like English 'y'. Short 'u'.

words.exampleNL: In juli gaan veel mensen op vakantie.
words.exampleEN: In July many people go on vacation.
words.audioHint: The 'j' sounds like English 'y'. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Augustus is meestal de warmste maand.
words.exampleEN: August is usually the warmest month.
words.audioHint: The 'au' sounds like 'ow'. The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: In september begint het nieuwe schooljaar.
words.exampleEN: In September the new school year begins.
words.audioHint: Very similar to English pronunciation.

words.exampleNL: In oktober worden de bladeren rood.
words.exampleEN: In October the leaves turn red.
words.audioHint: Similar to English. Short 'o' in the first syllable.

words.exampleNL: November is vaak grijs en regenachtig.
words.exampleEN: November is often grey and rainy.
words.audioHint: Very similar to English pronunciation.

words.exampleEN: In December we celebrate Christmas.
words.audioHint: The 'de' sounds like 'day'. The 'c' sounds like 's'.

words.exampleEN: It is three o'clock.
words.audioHint: Long 'uu' sound, like 'oo' in 'moon' but with lips pushed forward.

words.exampleNL: Ik ben over vijf minuten klaar.
words.exampleEN: I will be ready in five minutes.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: mi-NUUT. Long 'uu' sound.

words.exampleNL: Wacht even, nog een paar seconden.
words.exampleEN: Wait a moment, just a few more seconds.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: se-CON-de.

words.exampleEN: What are you doing today?
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: van-DAAG. Long 'aa'. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: Tomorrow I am going to Amsterdam.
words.audioHint: Short 'o'. The 'g' is guttural. Means both 'tomorrow' and 'morning'.

words.exampleEN: Yesterday the weather was nice.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. Stress on the first syllable.

words.exampleNL: Ik drink koffie in de ochtend.
words.exampleEN: I drink coffee in the morning.
words.audioHint: The 'ch' is guttural. Final 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleEN: We will see each other this afternoon.
words.audioHint: Short 'i'. Double 'd'. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: Good evening! How are you?
words.audioHint: Short 'a'. The 'v' is soft. Final 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleEN: Good night! Sleep well.
words.audioHint: Short 'a'. The 'ch' is guttural, then 't'.

words.exampleNL: Ik ga volgende week op vakantie.
words.exampleEN: I am going on vacation next week.
words.audioHint: The 'ee' sounds like 'ay' in 'say'.

words.exampleNL: Over twee maanden is het zomer.
words.exampleEN: In two months it will be summer.
words.audioHint: Long 'aa' sound. Final 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleNL: Ik woon hier al drie jaar.
words.exampleEN: I have been living here for three years.
words.audioHint: The 'j' sounds like English 'y'. Long 'aa' sound.

words.exampleEN: What a beautiful day!
words.audioHint: Short 'a'. The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: What are you doing this weekend?
words.audioHint: Similar to English. Final 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleEN: It is half past two. (Literally: half three)
words.audioHint: In Dutch, 'half drie' means 2:30, not 3:30! It means 'halfway to three'.

words.exampleEN: It is quarter past two.
words.audioHint: The 'kw' combination is crisp. Short 'a' sound.

words.exampleEN: It is ten past three.
words.audioHint: Short 'o'. Used for minutes past the hour.

words.exampleEN: It is ten to four.
words.audioHint: Long 'oo' sound (spelled 'oo'). Used for minutes before the hour.

words.exampleEN: I am getting up early tomorrow.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo'. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: Sorry, I am late.
words.audioHint: Long 'aa' sound. Final 't' is crisp.

words.exampleEN: I don't have time now.
words.audioHint: Short 'u' sound, like 'oo' in 'moon' but shorter.

words.exampleEN: I will call you back later.
words.audioHint: Short 'a' sound. The 'ks' at the end is crisp.

words.exampleNL: Ik ontbijt altijd om zeven uur.
words.exampleEN: I always have breakfast at seven o'clock.
words.audioHint: The 'ij' sounds like 'eye'. Final 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleNL: Ik ben nooit in Nederland geweest.
words.exampleEN: I have never been to the Netherlands.
words.audioHint: The 'oo' is long, then the 'i' makes it a diphthong: 'noy'.

words.exampleNL: Soms neem ik de bus naar mijn werk.
words.exampleEN: Sometimes I take the bus to work.
words.audioHint: Short 'o'. Very similar to English 'sums' but with 'o'.

words.exampleNL: We gaan vaak naar het park.
words.exampleEN: We often go to the park.
words.audioHint: Long 'aa' sound. The 'v' is soft.

words.exampleNL: Hoe laat is het op de klok?
words.exampleEN: What time is it on the clock?
words.audioHint: Short 'o' sound. Similar to English 'clock' without the 'c'.

words.exampleNL: Mijn horloge loopt twee minuten voor.
words.exampleEN: My watch is two minutes fast.
words.audioHint: French origin. The 'ge' at the end sounds like 'zhuh'.

words.exampleEN: What is the date today?
words.audioHint: Short 'a'. Stress on the first syllable.

words.exampleEN: My birthday is in May.
words.audioHint: Compound: 'ver' + 'jaar' (year) + 'dag' (day). The 'j' sounds like 'y'.

words.exampleEN: I only have one brother.
words.audioHint: When meaning 'one' (number), stress it: AYN. As 'a/an', it's unstressed: 'un'.

words.exampleEN: I want two bread rolls.
words.audioHint: The 'ee' sounds like 'ay'. The 'tw' is crisp.

words.exampleEN: We have three children.
words.audioHint: The 'ie' sounds like 'ee' in 'see'.

words.exampleEN: It is four o'clock in the afternoon.
words.audioHint: The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'. The 'v' is soft.

words.exampleNL: Over vijf minuten ben ik klaar.
words.exampleEN: In five minutes I will be ready.
words.audioHint: The 'ij' sounds like 'eye'. The 'v' is soft.

words.exampleEN: We eat at six o'clock.
words.audioHint: The 'z' is softer than in English. Short 'e'.

words.exampleNL: Ik sta op om zeven uur.
words.exampleEN: I get up at seven o'clock.
words.audioHint: The 'e' is long, like 'ay'. The 'v' is soft.

words.exampleNL: De winkel opent om acht uur.
words.exampleEN: The shop opens at eight o'clock.
words.audioHint: Short 'a'. The 'ch' is guttural, then 't'.

words.exampleNL: De les begint om negen uur.
words.exampleEN: The class starts at nine o'clock.
words.audioHint: Long 'e' like 'ay'. The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: It is ten o'clock in the evening.
words.audioHint: The 'ie' sounds like 'ee' in 'teen'.

words.exampleEN: I am twenty years old.
words.audioHint: Short 'i'. The 'w' is between 'v' and 'w'. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: The meeting lasts thirty minutes.
words.audioHint: Short 'e'. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Er waren meer dan honderd mensen.
words.exampleEN: There were more than a hundred people.
words.audioHint: Short 'o'. Final 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleEN: This house costs two hundred thousand euros.
words.audioHint: The 'ui' is the Dutch diphthong. Final 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleNL: Overmorgen ga ik naar de tandarts.
words.exampleEN: The day after tomorrow I am going to the dentist.
words.audioHint: Compound: 'over' + 'morgen'. The 'g' in 'morgen' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Eergisteren heb ik een film gezien.
words.exampleEN: The day before yesterday I watched a movie.
words.audioHint: Compound: 'eer' (before) + 'gisteren'. Long 'ee' then guttural 'g'.

words.exampleEN: What time does the movie start?
words.audioHint: Two words. 'Hoe' sounds like 'who'. 'Laat' has a long 'aa'.

words.exampleNL: Ik woon hier sinds twee jaar.
words.exampleEN: I have been living here since two years.
words.audioHint: The 'ds' at the end sounds like 'ts'.

words.exampleNL: De winkel is open tot zes uur.
words.exampleEN: The shop is open until six o'clock.
words.audioHint: Short 'o' sound. Very short word.

words.exampleEN: What are we eating tonight?
words.audioHint: Compound: 'van' + 'avond'. Final 'd' sounds like 't'.
